source: Decifer_MultiAccount_Architecture.docx — design complete, not yet built
top_3_quick_wins: Per-Account Config Profiles — single config.py change, backwards-compatible, unlocks everything else
Parameterise account_id through orders.py and risk.py — surgical audit, no behaviour change on single-account
Signal Extraction — decouple scan loop from execution, required before AccountDecisionAgent can exist

findings.title: Per-Account Config Profiles
findings.summary: Replace flat global CONFIG with an account-aware profile system. Each account ID maps to its own strategy type, risk params, score thresholds, and options settings. Global CONFIG is the fallback.
findings.expected_impact: Foundation for all multi-account work — blocks nothing after this
findings.what_changes: Add account_profiles dict to config.py. Keys are account IDs. Each entry holds: strategy_type, min_score_threshold, max_positions, risk_per_trade_pct, max_hold_time, stop_loss_rule, kill_switch_enabled, claude_api_key. Helper function get_account_config(account_id) merges profile over global defaults.
findings.why_it_matters: Every downstream change — agents, orders, risk — needs to ask 'which account?' This is the foundation. Without it nothing else can be per-account.

findings.feature: Parameterise account_id Through Orders and Risk
findings.title: Parameterise account_id Through Orders and Risk
findings.upgrades: orders.py + risk.py — replace CONFIG reads with account_id param
findings.summary: Audit every function reading CONFIG['active_account'] directly. Replace with account_id as an explicit required parameter. Today's behaviour is identical — the caller passes the single active account. Tomorrow it passes whichever account the agent is acting for.
findings.expected_impact: Makes orders and risk logic account-aware with zero behavioural change on current single-account setup
findings.what_changes: Grep for all CONFIG['active_account'] reads. For each: add account_id param to function signature, replace the CONFIG read. Update all callers in bot.py. Run tests after each file.
findings.why_it_matters: AccountDecisionAgent cannot exist until orders.py and risk.py accept an account_id. This is the critical path.

findings.feature: Extract Signal Objects from Brain Loop
findings.title: Extract Signal Objects from Brain Loop
findings.summary: Separate signal generation from execution. The scan loop produces typed Signal objects (symbol, direction, score, rationale, timestamp, options_flag) and passes them downstream. The brain does not execute — it just produces signals.
findings.expected_impact: Enables AccountDecisionAgent. Also enables WebSocket broadcasting in Phase 2.
findings.what_changes: Create signal_types.py with a Signal dataclass. Refactor the scan loop in bot.py so each scored opportunity is wrapped in a Signal. Remove all direct order calls from the scan loop.
findings.why_it_matters: The brain must not know about individual accounts. Signal objects are the clean interface between brain and agents.

findings.title: AccountDecisionAgent Class
findings.summary: New agent class downstream of the 6-agent pipeline. One instance per IBKR sub-account. Receives Signal objects, applies the account's score threshold and strategy type filter, and executes or discards using the account's own Claude API key — never the global key.
findings.expected_impact: The core innovation — enables fully independent per-account execution from a shared brain
findings.what_changes: Create AccountDecisionAgent in account_agents.py. __init__ takes an account_profile. evaluate(signal) checks: strategy type match, score >= threshold, max_positions headroom, kill_switch state. On pass: calls into orders.py with account_id. Wire three instances (options, longs, scalping) into the bot.py scan loop.
findings.why_it_matters: This is what makes Decifer a multi-account platform rather than a multi-account bot. Each agent is sovereign.

findings.title: Connection Manager — Multi-Account IBKR
findings.summary: Manages multiple ib_insync IB() instances, one per IBKR sub-account, each with a unique client ID. Per-account reconnection, heartbeats, and subscription restoration — one disconnected account never stalls others.
findings.expected_impact: Makes multi-account IBKR connections robust — essential before any live trading
findings.what_changes: ConnectionManager holds a dict of account_id -> IB(). connect_all() starts each with unique client_id. reconnect(account_id) retries with exponential backoff (1s, 2s, 4s, 8s). Heartbeat loop pings each connection every 30s. Refactor bot.py to use ConnectionManager.
findings.why_it_matters: ib_insync requires unique client IDs per connection. Without this, multiple accounts on the same machine will conflict and disconnect each other.

findings.title: Per-Account Local Dashboard
findings.summary: Update the local dashboard (port 8080) to show positions, P&L, and trade history broken out per account rather than one aggregate view. Shows which signals each agent accepted vs discarded.
findings.expected_impact: Gives visibility into all three accounts without switching screens
findings.what_changes: Query positions per account_id from ConnectionManager. Render three side-by-side panels: options, longs, scalping. Each shows live positions, today P&L, last 5 trades with rationale snippet, agent accept/discard log.
findings.why_it_matters: You cannot run three accounts blind. This is the first visibility layer before cloud infrastructure exists.

findings.title: Cloud VPS Setup and Deployment
findings.summary: Provision a cloud VPS (Ubuntu 22, Docker) hosting the shared brain container and all per-user containers. Configure firewall, DNS, SSL, IBKR Gateway headless mode. Estimated cost at 50 users: $350–500/month.
findings.expected_impact: Moves the brain to always-on cloud — prerequisite for Phase 2 features
findings.what_changes: Choose provider (DigitalOcean, Hetzner, or AWS — open question). Provision Ubuntu 22 with Docker and nginx. Set up headless IBC. Configure ufw firewall. Write reproducible deploy.sh bootstrap script.
findings.why_it_matters: The WebSocket broadcaster and per-user containers require a cloud host. This is the Phase 2 prerequisite.

findings.title: WebSocket Signal Broadcaster
findings.summary: The brain publishes every Signal to all connected containers in real time via WebSocket. Each signal carries a sequence number. The brain has no knowledge of who receives it — it just broadcasts.
findings.expected_impact: Enables real-time signal delivery to unlimited user containers simultaneously
findings.what_changes: WebSocket server using FastAPI + WebSockets. On each scan cycle, serialise Signals to JSON and broadcast to all clients. Include sequence_num on every message. Add /health endpoint.
findings.why_it_matters: The multi-account model depends on all containers receiving signals simultaneously from one source. Without this, each container would need its own scanner.

findings.feature: Reconnection Logic — Backoff, Sequence, State Sync
findings.title: Reconnection Logic
findings.summary: Resilient container-to-brain connections: exponential backoff (1s, 2s, 4s, 8s), sequence number gap detection, and signal snapshot on reconnect so no signals are missed.
findings.expected_impact: Prevents missed trades on network interruption — critical for production reliability
findings.what_changes: Exponential backoff on container side. On reconnect, container sends last_seen_sequence. Brain replays signals since that sequence from a rolling 5-minute buffer. 30-second ping/pong heartbeat.
findings.why_it_matters: Missed connections mean missed signals and missed trades. This is the reliability layer for the WebSocket architecture.

findings.title: Per-User Docker Compose Containers
findings.summary: Each user runs in an isolated Docker container with their own account agents, IBKR Gateway, and encrypted credential store. One container breach means zero risk to other users.
findings.expected_impact: Enables the platform to serve multiple users in complete isolation
findings.what_changes: docker-compose.user.yml template with decifer-agent + ibkr-gateway services. Python Fernet encryption for credentials. provision_user.sh script. Credentials decrypted only at execution time. Immutable audit log for all credential access.
findings.why_it_matters: Shared containers mean shared risk. Per-container encryption is the security guarantee that makes this platform trustworthy.

findings.feature: Web Dashboard — Live Positions and P&L
findings.title: Web Dashboard
findings.summary: Web dashboard each user can open in any browser. Live positions and P&L per account, trade history with AI reasoning, strategy settings, and connection status.
findings.expected_impact: Gives each user a professional-grade view of their portfolio from anywhere
findings.what_changes: FastAPI + Jinja2 or React (open question). state_publisher.py pushes positions per-container to dashboard. Trade history from trades.jsonl log. Connection status panel polls brain /health. Deploy behind nginx with HTTPS.
findings.why_it_matters: Users cannot manage accounts they cannot see. The web dashboard is the primary day-to-day interface.

findings.feature: Telegram Bot — Notifications and Quick Commands
findings.title: Telegram Bot
findings.summary: Trade notifications pushed when a trade executes with brief AI rationale. Morning briefing at 09:20 ET. Quick commands: /pause, /resume, /positions, /pnl. Never used for order execution.
findings.expected_impact: Keeps users informed without requiring them to open a dashboard
findings.what_changes: python-telegram-bot or aiogram. One bot, per-user chat_id in strategy profile. On trade execute: push notification with symbol, direction, price, rationale (max 280 chars). Morning briefing job at 09:20 ET. /pause sets kill_switch=True.
findings.why_it_matters: Users want to know when their money moves. Telegram is the lightest possible notification layer — no app install needed.
